summary.summary: This week's notable submissions skew toward safety evaluation and inference-time controls rather than novel alignment theory, with two new domain-specific safety benchmarks (EMPATH for multilingual emotional-support chatbots and CAREBench for child-safety risk recognition) standing out by targeting failure modes that fixed-prompt, single-turn benchmarks miss. Topic counts remain dominated by Scalable Oversight (712) and Mechanistic Interpretability (692), but the concretely notable work is mostly applied: safe diffusion sampling via concept removal (2606.29801), interpretability diagnostics via observable matrix dynamics (2606.29679), and privacy/reconstruction attacks on GNNs (2606.29748). A large share of flagged 'notable' papers are actually capability/applications work (GUI grounding, dance video generation, vision-language agents), suggesting a relatively thin week for frontier safety results despite high raw topic counts. The clearest signal is a push toward multi-turn, multilingual, and pre-escalation safety evaluation, implying growing recognition that current benchmarks under-measure realistic conversational harm.
themes: Multi-turn / multilingual evaluation beyond fixed-prompt settings
Inference-time safety controls for generative models
Privacy and reconstruction attacks on neural models
Capability-heavy week (agents, GUI grounding, video generation)

notable.why_it_matters: EMPATH targets a real blind spot by evaluating emotional-support chatbot safety across multilingual, multi-turn crisis conversations rather than the fixed single-prompt setups most benchmarks rely on.
CAREBench shifts child-safety evaluation upstream of explicit CSAM to earlier model assistance in manipulation and impersonation, addressing a pre-escalation failure mode in frontier systems.
Concept Removal Guidance offers an evidence-calibrated inference-time control to block disallowed text-to-image content while reducing the safety-fidelity tradeoff of fixed-weight negative guidance.

Observable Matrix Dynamics provides a random-matrix-theory diagnostic for how networks' internal representations evolve, a potential interpretability tool for distinguishing smooth learning from phase transitions.
Rethinking generative reconstruction attacks on GNNs is relevant to privacy risk for models trained on sensitive non-Euclidean graph data.

Confidence-driven test-time RL for vision-language-action models pushes autonomy capability by removing reliance on external success signals, raising oversight questions for self-directed policy updates.
Dynamo's training-free skill-tool evolution lets a frozen VLM self-adapt, illustrating a low-cost path to agentic self-improvement worth monitoring for control implications.

risks.risk: Training-free, confidence-driven test-time RL on robot/embodied (VLA) policies lets deployed physical agents rewrite their own behavior using self-generated reward signals — outside any weight-update audit trail and outside the bounded-task monitoring that physical-safety regulators actually run.
risks.research_anchor: Quarterly divergence: Agentic / Autonomy Capability +39.2%/qtr vs. Agentic Behavior Monitoring +13.8%/qtr (NEW safety-lag alert this week); cluster of THIS-WEEK papers using training-free self-improvement on embodied agents.
risks.derived_from: New safety-lag alert (Agentic Autonomy vs Agentic Behavior Monitoring); 'Trust Your Instincts: Confidence-Driven Test-Time RL for Vision-Language-Action Models' (2606.29892, RL/RLHF + Self-Improvement, no external success signal); 'Dynamo: Dynamic Skill-Tool Evolution for Vision-Language Agents' (2606.30185, training-free, frozen VLM adapts itself); 'Towards Continual Motion-Language Agents: LoRA Variants' (2606.30266, incremental motion generation).
risks.communities: VLA / embodied-robotics researchers see the capability half (they celebrate that a robot can improve its grasp policy at test time with no labels and no external reward). Functional-safety / robotics-certification engineers (ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066, machinery directive assessors) and CAISI's frontier-eval teams see the deployment-safety half but assume the policy is FROZEN after certification. They don't connect because robot-cert culture has no concept of a controller that re-derives its own reward online from its own confidence, and the VLA papers frame it as a benign efficiency win, not a change to the safety-case assumption.
risks.framing_inversion: Test-time RL without external feedback is sold as SAFER and cheaper (no risky exploration, no human reward labels). Inverted: removing the external success signal also removes the external observability — the very absence that makes it 'self-sufficient' is what makes its drift invisible to a safety auditor who was relying on logged reward signals as the monitoring surface.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Self-improvement-risk discourse is almost entirely about disembodied LLM agents and snapshot-evasion of evals; nobody has tied the confidence-driven test-time RL trend to PHYSICAL safety cases, where the governing assumption is a frozen controller. The embodied papers are a few days old and read as efficiency improvements.
risks.mechanism: A VLA policy certified on a frozen behavior distribution is deployed; confidence-driven test-time RL (2606.29892) updates the policy online using self-generated reward, and skill-tool evolution (2606.30185) lets it acquire new action primitives. The deployed behavior distribution drifts away from the one that was safety-tested. Because the reward is internal and no weight-update log is mandated, the drift is unobservable to the certifier, and monitoring designed for bounded, single-output tasks does not flag emergent multi-step action sequences.
risks.leading_indicator: First incident report or recall in which a deployed robot/agent's behavior is found to have changed post-deployment via on-device adaptation with no corresponding firmware/weight update record; or a robotics vendor system card disclosing 'continuous test-time adaptation' without an accompanying re-certification protocol.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence on the research trend (multiple concrete THIS-WEEK papers + a fresh safety-lag alert), but the leap from 'method exists in research' to 'shipped in a certified physical product within 24 months' is inferential — I am NOT claiming deployment is imminent. The claim that current robot-safety certification assumes frozen controllers is well-grounded in standards practice; the claim that monitoring 'will miss' the drift is an inference, lowered accordingly.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed these test-time-RL methods migrate from research VLAs into commercially deployed embodied systems and that certification regimes will not pre-emptively require on-device adaptation logging — neither is shown in the digest.
risks.score_rationale: Severity high because physical-world drift in certified robots is hard to reverse and safety-critical; likelihood lowered to 2 because commercial deployment of self-modifying certified controllers within 24 months is inferential; exposure 3 (industrial/service robotics is significant but not yet ubiquitous); trajectory accelerating per the +39.2%/qtr capability growth and the new safety-lag alert.

verification.prior_coverage: The individual elements are already publicly active: test-time RL on VLA policies is a named, fast-moving research line (e.g. 'On-the-Fly VLA Adaptation via Test-Time Reinforcement Learning'/TT-VLA, verifier-free test-time sampling for VLAs), deployment-time distribution shift for robot policies is the subject of dedicated surveys, and 'AI agent behavioural drift' is explicitly framed as a governance/monitoring gap. The 'locked vs. continuously-learning' certification problem is a mature, well-documented debate in the FDA medical-device context and is now being raised against industrial robot standards (ISO 10218-1/-2:2025). What is comparatively fresh is the precise stitch: confidence-driven, self-rewarded test-time RL on a physical, safety-certified VLA producing drift that sits outside both weight-update audit trails and bounded-task monitoring.

verification.disputed_claims: The load-bearing premise — that a self-generated/confidence signal is a usable reward that meaningfully improves behavior — is actively contested in the test-time RL literature. Work on inference-time/test-time reward hacking, entropy/length-variance collapse (ECHO; confidence-conditioned verification), reward collapse, and demonstrated overconfidence/poor calibration of self-rewarding and process reward models (Agentic Overconfidence; PRM overconfidence; LLM overprecision studies) shows confidence-driven self-reward can degrade or destabilize rather than safely improve a policy. This cuts BOTH ways: it weakens any 'it will reliably self-improve into new competent-but-unsafe behavior' framing, while it strengthens the safety concern that unsupervised confidence-driven updates can drift unpredictably. Separately, the claim that current robot-safety certification 'assumes frozen controllers' is broadly consistent with the locked/adaptive debate (well established for FDA software, emerging for ISO 10218-1/-2:2025), but the standards sources do not explicitly state a blanket frozen-controller mandate, so that step is inferred rather than directly evidenced.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower confidence than the candidate states. The component trends are real and current (test-time RL on VLAs, deployment distribution shift, agent behavioral drift as a monitoring gap, locked-vs-adaptive certification), but each is already in public discussion, so the risk should be briefed as a synthesis/seam, not a novel discovery — only the specific framing (self-rewarded test-time RL on a certified PHYSICAL VLA escaping both weight-update audit trails and bounded-task monitoring) is comparatively fresh. Crucially, the risk's mechanism leans on confidence-driven self-reward being effective, yet that exact assumption is disputed: the literature documents reward hacking, entropy collapse, and overconfident/miscalibrated self-reward, so the threat model must allow that such updates may produce erratic/degraded drift rather than coherent capability gain — which still poses a safety problem but undermines a confident 'agent will competently rewrite itself' narrative. The 'frozen-controller certification' premise is plausible but partly inferential. Net: keep as an Emerging, LOW-to-moderate-confidence seam to monitor; do not brief as a confident or novel claim, and explicitly carry the dispute over self-reward reliability and the inference about certification scope.

risks.risk: Government and statutory frontier evaluations are being designed around single-output/bounded-task safety while frontier deployment moves to long-horizon agentic behavior — institutionalizing an evaluation paradigm that systematically misses the failure mode it most needs to catch.
risks.research_anchor: Agentic Behavior Monitoring is THIS WEEK's new safety-lag alert (Agentic/Autonomy Capability vs. Agentic Behavior Monitoring); quarterly backdrop shows cap growth +39.2%/qtr vs. monitoring +13.8%/qtr — monitoring is growing but losing the race, and was flagged as a NEW alert this week.
risks.derived_from: new_safety_lag_alerts: Agentic/Autonomy Capability vs Agentic Behavior Monitoring; divergence pairing (39.2 vs 13.8); EMPATH benchmark (2606.30256) explicitly critiques benchmarks that 'buy scalability by fixing the prompt, language and turn structure'; CAISI May 5 pre-deployment agreements and Great American AI Act semi-annual evaluation requirement.
risks.communities: CAISI/NIST evaluators and statutory drafters (SB 53, Great American AI Act) see the 'mandate periodic evaluation' half and are racing to operationalize it; agentic-monitoring researchers (EMPATH's multi-turn auditor-judge critique, Dynamo's self-evolving tool agents) see that bounded single-turn evals miss where multi-step agentic failures emerge. They are not connecting because eval-mandate design is happening on policy/legal timelines and the methodological critique is buried in fresh benchmark papers with no policy uptake yet.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: mandated pre-deployment evaluation is straightforwardly a safety win. Inversion: a mandate that codifies a bounded-task evaluation methodology can lock in a measurement paradigm blind to long-horizon agentic failure, manufacturing false assurance and crowding out the harder monitoring work.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The eval-mandate story is reported as a governance victory; the methodological critique that current benchmarks fix prompt/turn structure is this-week academic news with no bridge to the policy conversation. Nobody is asking whether the eval methods the statutes will reference can even see agentic failure.
risks.mechanism: Statutory and CAISI evaluations will reference whatever benchmark suites are mature and citable now — overwhelmingly single-output or short-turn. As deployment shifts to self-evolving, multi-tool, long-horizon agents (Dynamo, motion-language agents), the evaluation surface diverges from the deployment surface. Passing the mandated eval becomes a compliance and liability shield while the actual risk locus goes unmeasured.
risks.leading_indicator: CAISI evaluation protocols, SB 53 risk-framework disclosures, or the Great American AI Act semi-annual eval spec citing fixed-prompt/single-turn benchmark suites without long-horizon agentic-trajectory test requirements; OR a deployed agent failure that passed its mandated eval.
risks.calibration: Emerging, medium confidence. The research signal (new this-week alert + EMPATH's explicit critique) is solid. The causal claim that mandates will ossify around bounded evals is inferential — eval standards are still being drafted and could incorporate agentic methods. I lower likelihood accordingly; the harm is a slow-building assurance gap, not an acute event.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed that statutory/CAISI evals will lean on currently-mature benchmark suites and that those are predominantly bounded-task; the digest does not confirm which suites regulators will adopt.
risks.score_rationale: New this-week safety-lag alert with capability outpacing monitoring (39.2 vs 13.8) and active statutory eval mandates being drafted makes this broad and accelerating; likelihood held to 3 because eval-standard ossification is inferential and still preventable.

verification.prior_coverage: The risk's core mechanism — that static, single-output/short-turn benchmarks fail to capture long-horizon, multi-tool agentic behavior — is already extensively discussed across mainstream policy outlets (Brookings) and a large 2025-2026 academic literature (OSWorld, ATBench, LPS-Bench, OpenAgentSafety, Tool Decathlon, surveys), with explicit framing that 'AI systems are almost never used the way they are benchmarked.' The adjacent governance seam — that passing a mandated eval becomes a compliance/liability shield while real risk goes unmeasured — is also published (arXiv internal-deployment-gaps, third-party compliance reviews, FLI AI Safety Index on developer-controlled evals). The specific tie to CAISI pre-deployment agreements and the Great American AI Act semi-annual requirement is fresher, but the underlying critique it depends on is mainstream.

verification.disputed_claims: Two layers are contested. (1) The risk's foundational premise — that benchmarks 'systematically miss' the real failure mode — rests on the construct-validity critique of evals, which is itself a live, unsettled debate: Raji et al. and the EC/JRC review argue many benchmarks 'do not measure what they claim to measure,' while others treat evals as imperfect-but-necessary evidence (e.g., SecureBio's biorisk evidence-hierarchy view). So the premise is well-supported but not uncontested fact; how badly evals fail, and whether that gap is 'systematic,' is disputed among measurement researchers. (2) The risk's distinctive causal claim — that statutory/CAISI mandates WILL ossify around bounded benchmarks — is inferential and unverified: AISI/NIST and the international evaluation community are actively building agentic and autonomy evaluations, so the assumption that mandates freeze around single-output evals is contested by the direction of current standards work, not confirmed by it.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower confidence and reframe from 'novel risk' to 'known gap with a fresher policy hook.' The technical core (static/short-turn benchmarks under-measure long-horizon agentic behavior) is already prominent in policy and academic discourse and should NOT be briefed as unsurfaced. The genuinely fresher element is narrow: the specific coupling of this known eval-deployment gap to named US statutory instruments (CAISI pre-deployment agreements, Great American AI Act semi-annual evaluation requirement) and whether they will hard-code citable-but-bounded suites. Even that hook must carry two caveats: (a) the premise depends on the contested construct-validity critique, where the severity of the 'miss' is disputed among measurement researchers, so it cannot be laundered into a confident claim; and (b) the load-bearing causal step — that mandates ossify around bounded evals — runs against observable evidence that AISI/NIST are actively developing agentic/autonomy evaluations, making it an inferential bet rather than a documented trend. Net: surface only as a watch-item flag on standards-drafting language, not as a high-confidence novel risk; harm remains a slow-building, conditional assurance gap.

risks.risk: Test-time, training-free self-improvement methods (no weight updates, self-generated reward signals) let agents acquire new capabilities AFTER passing pre-deployment evaluation, structurally defeating the snapshot logic on which CAISI and statutory evals depend.
risks.research_anchor: Self-Improvement / RSI Capability appears in three of this week's notable papers (Dynamo 2606.30185, motion-language LoRA 2606.30266, VLA confidence-driven test-time RL 2606.29892) — and crucially these are training-free / test-time methods; quarterly Self-Improvement/RSI capability growth is among the fastest while Self-Improvement Risk & Control safety topic sits at only 145 papers/week.
risks.derived_from: 2606.30185 Dynamo ('training-free framework that adapts a frozen VLM without any weight updates'); 2606.29892 ('Confidence-Driven Test-Time RL' without external reward signal); 2606.30266 (continual incremental skill acquisition via LoRA); CAISI pre-deployment testing agreements and Great American AI Act semi-annual (not continuous) evaluation cadence.
risks.communities: Policy/eval designers (CAISI, statutory drafters) see the 'evaluate a fixed model snapshot pre-deployment, re-test semi-annually' half and treat capability as a fixed property of weights. Capability researchers building test-time/training-free self-improvement (Dynamo, confidence-driven test-time RL) see that a deployed agent can gain skills with no weight change and no retraining event. They don't connect because the eval framework assumes capability = function of weights, while these methods make capability = function of runtime context the eval never observes.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: 'we evaluated the model, capability is bounded.' Inversion: training-free self-improvement means the evaluated artifact and the deployed agent can diverge in capability with zero detectable model-version change — the entire pre-deployment-snapshot premise is undercut without anyone changing the weights regulators signed off on.
risks.why_underdiscussed: RSI risk discourse focuses on dramatic recursive self-improvement and weight-modifying training loops. The quietly significant move this week is mundane training-free test-time adaptation — framed as efficiency/convenience, not as an evaluation-validity problem. The compliance implication (your signed-off snapshot is not what runs) is invisible to both sides.
risks.mechanism: An agent passes pre-deployment eval as a frozen model. In deployment it uses test-time RL / self-evolving tools / incremental LoRA to acquire new behaviors triggered by context. No weight update, no new model version, no re-evaluation trigger fires. Capability relevant to dual-use harm (agentic misuse, tool chaining) can emerge between the semi-annual checkpoints, inside the assurance window.
risks.leading_indicator: A documented case where a deployed agent exhibits a dangerous-capability behavior absent from its pre-deployment eval, attributable to test-time/in-context adaptation rather than a model update; OR regulators/labs publicly debating whether training-free adaptation triggers re-evaluation obligations.
risks.calibration: Emerging, medium confidence. The research signal is strong and this-week-specific (three fresh papers on training-free adaptation). The leap to 'defeats regulatory snapshot logic' is inferential — current test-time methods yield modest, task-specific gains, not arbitrary capability acquisition; I lower severity/likelihood to reflect that these are gains-on-known-tasks, not unbounded uplift.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed test-time/training-free adaptation can produce capability changes material enough to matter for safety mandates; the papers demonstrate task-performance gains, not safety-relevant capability jumps.
risks.score_rationale: Three distinct this-week papers on training-free self-improvement against a thin (145/wk) safety control topic and a semi-annual eval cadence make the seam real and broad; likelihood/severity held at 3 because current methods yield bounded task gains, not demonstrated dangerous-capability emergence.

verification.prior_coverage: The exact seam — that the 'Train→Freeze→Evaluate→Deploy' snapshot model breaks once systems learn or adapt at deployment time — is already publicly and prominently articulated, most directly by an Oxford Martin AIGI blog post titled 'When AI Systems Learn During Deployment, Our Safety Evaluations Break,' which explicitly notes that an institute testing a model is 'testing a fixed object' and that the 'freeze' step is doing heavy load-bearing work. The broader eval–deployment gap, post-deployment evaluation cadence, and regulation of models that evolve in their lifecycle are also actively discussed (arXiv 'Internal Deployment Gaps in AI Regulation', IDAIS-Oxford red-lines statement, radiology-AI lifecycle regulation, multiple benchmark-limitation pieces). The specific regulatory hooks named in the risk (CAISI agreements, the 'Great American AI Act' cadence) are not themselves found in coverage, but the structural critique they instantiate is well-trodden.

verification.disputed_claims: The load-bearing dependency — that training-free / test-time methods actually confer real, transferable new capability (rather than modest, task-local, or illusory gains) — is contested in the literature. 'The Illusion of Progress?' (NeurIPS 2025, arXiv 2506.24000) argues test-time adaptation work suffers from duplicated baselines, inconsistent settings, and overstated practical value; 'Mirage of Test-Time Scaling' (arXiv 2506.04210) finds extended test-time thinking produces an 'illusion of improved reasoning' via increased output variance rather than genuine gains. Separately, the risk's 'self-generated reward signal' assumption is undercut by work showing test-time RL faces unreliable reward estimation without supervision and is prone to reward hacking (ETTRL arXiv 2508.11356; Inference-Time Reward Hacking). So both the 'capability acquisition' premise and the 'reliable self-reward' premise are disputed by active researchers.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'Emerging, medium confidence' to 'Already-surfaced framing, low novelty, low–medium confidence on the load-bearing mechanism.' The core seam (snapshot/frozen-model evaluation breaks when systems adapt at deployment) is NOT novel — it is the explicit thesis of a public Oxford Martin AIGI piece and adjacent regulatory/lifecycle-evaluation literature, so it should not be briefed as newly discovered. What is comparatively fresh is the specific tie to this week's training-free papers and to named statutory cadences (CAISI / semi-annual 'Great American AI Act' checkpoints), but that specificity does not make the underlying risk new. Critically, the risk's confident step from 'training-free adaptation exists' to 'agents acquire dual-use-relevant capability inside the assurance window' rests on a CONTESTED claim: multiple 2025 papers argue test-time gains are modest, task-local, or illusory, and that self-generated reward signals are unreliable and reward-hackable. The original calibration already hedges toward 'gains-on-known-tasks, not unbounded uplift'; honest recalibration goes further — the mechanism's severity is bounded by an empirically disputed premise, so it should be presented as a governance-design caveat (continuous vs. snapshot assurance) with the dispute stated up front, not as a confident structural defeat of regulatory evals.

risks.risk: Child-safety and emotional-support chatbot benchmarks are arriving precisely as child safety is carved OUT of federal preemption — turning a thin, fresh research literature into the de facto legal standard before it is mature, with no cross-lingual deployment validation.
risks.research_anchor: Two this-week benchmark papers in the Evals & Dangerous-Capability Benchmarks / Deceptive Alignment cluster — CAREBench (2606.29685, child-safety risk) and EMPATH (2606.30256, multilingual auditor-judge for emotional-support chatbots) — appearing while the overall Evals topic is small (101/week) and Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness sits at only 99/week.
risks.derived_from: 2606.29685 CAREBench ('child-safety failures begin earlier: in model assistance that helps adults manipulate, impersonate'); 2606.30256 EMPATH (multilingual multi-turn crisis-conversation safety, critiquing fixed-prompt benchmarks); SOCIETAL_CONTEXT: Dec 2025 EO and Great American AI Act both EXEMPT child safety from preemption; EU Digital Omnibus adds Article 5 CSAM/NCII prohibition (Dec 2, 2026).
risks.communities: Child-safety/family-law policymakers and the carve-out drafters (EO 14365, federal draft, EU Article 5) see the 'child safety must be regulated, not preempted' half and need a measurable standard fast. The chatbot-safety benchmark researchers (CAREBench, EMPATH) see how immature and English/Western-centric current child-safety and emotional-support evals are. They don't connect because regulators urgently need a citable benchmark and will grab the first available one, unaware EMPATH itself argues existing benchmarks miss multilingual multi-turn failures.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: child-safety carve-outs and new benchmarks are an unambiguous protective good. Inversion: because child safety is the ONE domain everyone agrees should not be preempted, it becomes the fastest path to law — and the most immature, narrow benchmark can be frozen into binding standard before its blind spots (multilingual, multi-turn, manipulation-not-just-CSAM) are characterized, harming the children it aims to protect via false assurance.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The child-safety carve-out is celebrated as the rare bipartisan consensus; nobody is examining whether the measurement science behind it exists. CAREBench's own framing — that failures begin in adult-manipulation assistance, not just CSAM — shows current evals look at the wrong layer, but this is hours-old research with no policy bridge.
risks.mechanism: Child safety is exempted from preemption across EO, federal draft, and EU Article 5 — creating intense demand for an operational standard. Regulators and platforms adopt the earliest mature-looking benchmark. CAREBench/EMPATH reveal the live frontier (multilingual crisis conversations, manipulation-enablement) is exactly what fixed-prompt benchmarks miss. A narrow standard becomes the compliance bar; vendors optimize to it; non-English and multi-turn manipulation failures persist unmeasured.
risks.leading_indicator: A regulator, platform child-safety policy, or the EU Article 5 implementing guidance referencing a specific child-safety chatbot benchmark as the conformance bar without requiring multilingual/multi-turn coverage; OR a documented harm in a non-English emotional-support chatbot interaction that passed an adopted English-centric benchmark.
risks.calibration: Emerging, medium-low confidence. Anchor is strong (two fresh, on-point benchmark papers + a real, dated carve-out structure). The harm chain depends on regulators adopting an immature benchmark as binding — plausible given the urgency but not yet observed; I lower likelihood. Severity is elevated because the affected population is children.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed regulators will operationalize child-safety carve-outs via specific benchmarks soon and that the available benchmarks remain English/Western-centric; the digest shows the benchmarks exist but not that any has been adopted into law.
risks.score_rationale: Two precise this-week benchmarks intersecting a real child-safety carve-out give a high-severity, vulnerable-population seam; likelihood held to 2 because regulatory adoption of an immature standard is plausible but unobserved, and the overall evals topic volume (101/wk) is modest.

verification.prior_coverage: Every component of this risk is already publicly and prominently discussed. The child-safety carve-out from federal AI preemption is documented across the Dec 11 2025 EO and dozens of legal analyses; child-safety LLM benchmarks (MinorBench, Safe-Child-LLM, and others) form a fast-growing rather than negligible literature; the danger that immature benchmarks become a gamed de facto regulatory standard is itself the explicit thesis of work like 'Beyond Benchmarks: The False Promise of AI Regulation' and benchmark-flaw audits; and multilingual/non-English safety failures are extensively reported. What is somewhat fresher is the precise synthesis tying the carve-out's urgency to a specific, still-thin child/crisis benchmark becoming binding while cross-lingual and multi-turn failures go unmeasured.

verification.disputed_claims: Two load-bearing assumptions are weak or contested. (1) The premise that child-safety benchmark literature is 'thin/fresh' is overstated: a sizeable benchmark body already exists (MinorBench, Safe-Child-LLM, CRADLE-Bench, MLCommons AI Safety Benchmark), so the 'immature literature' framing is disputable. (2) The core mechanism — regulators adopting a single benchmark as the binding de facto legal standard — is NOT observed; current 2026 state chatbot laws (e.g., NY S9051B, CA, and ~78 bills across 27 states) legislate conduct rules (disclosure, anti-sycophancy, crisis-referral, age assurance) rather than ratifying a named benchmark, and legal commentators note the EO's preemption pathway itself faces years of litigation and uncertain authority (state AGs, Gibson Dunn, Latham). Separately, AI-governance researchers ('Beyond Benchmarks') already dispute that benchmarks can serve as reliable regulatory standards at all — which both supports the harm and undercuts the novelty.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower to low confidence and reframe as a synthesis of already-prominent threads rather than an unsurfaced risk. The carve-out, the proliferation of child-safety benchmarks, the multilingual-failure gap, and the general 'benchmarks make a bad regulatory standard / get gamed' critique are each front-page or established in the literature; bundling them is a useful framing but not novel. The specific causal claim the risk depends on — a still-immature benchmark hardening into THE binding compliance bar — is currently unobserved and arguably contradicted by how 2026 chatbot-safety laws are actually being drafted (conduct-based, not benchmark-ratifying), and by the contested, litigation-bound status of the preemption scheme itself. The genuinely fresh and defensible angle is narrower: that whatever operational standard emerges may under-test non-English and multi-turn manipulation failures. Severity remains elevated because children are the affected population, but likelihood should be marked down given the disputed 'thin literature' premise and the unproven benchmark-as-law mechanism.

risks.risk: Multilingual auditor-judge safety benchmarks (LLM-grades-LLM) for emotional-support and child-safety chatbots are arriving exactly as the safety-research subfield they depend on (Scalable Oversight) DECELERATES — meaning the 'judge' models doing multi-turn crisis grading rest on an oversight foundation whose methodological scrutiny is shrinking just as these benchmarks get drafted into the one legally-carved-out domain (child safety).
risks.research_anchor: Quarterly divergence: Multi-agent/tool-use capability +21.8%/qtr vs. Scalable Oversight -5.4%/qtr (oversight DECELERATING) — the exact safety subfield underpinning auditor-judge evaluation pipelines.
risks.derived_from: Decelerating Scalable Oversight (-5.4%/qtr) co-occurring with THIS-WEEK auditor-judge safety benchmarks tagged to Scalable Oversight: 'EMPATH: A Multilingual Auditor-Judge Benchmark for Safety Evaluation of Emotional-Support Chatbots' (2606.30256) and 'CAREBench: A Child-Safety Risk Benchmark for Language Models' (2606.29685), both tagging Scalable Oversight + Deceptive Alignment + Evals. Societal: child safety is explicitly EXEMPTED from federal preemption (EO 14365); CAISI semi-annual eval mandate in draft Great American AI Act.
risks.communities: Applied-safety-benchmark builders (EMPATH/CAREBench authors) see the deployment half: they need scalable, multilingual graders and reach for auditor-judge (model-grades-model) designs because human grading of multi-turn crisis dialogue doesn't scale. Scalable-oversight METHODS researchers see the foundations half — the open problems in judge reliability, judge gameability, and self-grading bias — but that methods literature is shrinking (-5.4%/qtr). They are not connecting because benchmark authors treat 'use a strong model as judge' as a settled engineering primitive, not as an unsolved oversight research problem; and oversight researchers are not tracking which carved-out legal domains are about to harden their methods into standards.
risks.framing_inversion: Auditor-judge benchmarks are framed as the responsible, scalable way to evaluate sensitive chatbots (better than no eval). Inverted: scaling the evaluation by automating the judge imports an UNSOLVED-and-now-under-researched oversight problem (judge unreliability/gameability) directly into the safety floor — and because child safety is the one preemption-exempt domain, a thin judge-based standard can crystallize into the de facto legal benchmark precisely where methodological scrutiny is fading, not rising.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The already-considered seams flagged the child-safety preemption carve-out and the immaturity of the benchmark literature — but NOT the specific structural coupling: that these benchmarks lean on auditor-JUDGE (scalable-oversight) machinery whose underlying research velocity is going NEGATIVE this quarter. The judge-reliability dependency is invisible because each community owns only one side.
risks.mechanism: EMPATH and CAREBench operationalize safety grading via model-as-auditor/judge over multi-turn, multilingual dialogue. Their validity inherits the (contested, under-studied) reliability of scalable-oversight judging. As scalable-oversight research decelerates, fewer new results stress-test judge bias, cross-lingual judge degradation, and judge gameability. Meanwhile child-safety evaluation is the carved-out domain regulators and CAISI can act on; a fresh, judge-based benchmark becomes the reference standard. A frontier model can then be tuned to satisfy the JUDGE rather than the underlying safety property, and the deficiency goes unexamined because the methods community that would catch it is shrinking.
risks.leading_indicator: An auditor-judge benchmark (EMPATH/CAREBench lineage) cited in a CAISI evaluation protocol, a state child-safety rule, or a lab system card as the child-safety standard — WITHOUT a published judge-reliability / judge-gameability validation across the benchmark's claimed languages.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence. The research-trend anchor (Scalable Oversight -5.4%/qtr) is this tool's proprietary signal and is treated as an ATTENTION signal, not a causal claim that judges are getting worse — I explicitly do NOT assert judge quality is declining, only that scrutiny of it is. The legal-crystallization step depends on the draft Great American AI Act / CAISI mandate, which are not yet law; likelihood lowered accordingly.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed EMPATH/CAREBench rely on model-as-judge grading (strongly implied by 'auditor-judge' in the EMPATH title and the Scalable Oversight tag, but full methodology not in the digest) and that regulators will reach for fresh academic benchmarks as standards — neither fully shown.
risks.score_rationale: Severity 3 (faulty child-safety floor harms a vulnerable population but is correctable); likelihood 3 (benchmark-to-standard pathway is plausible given the carve-out but legally unsettled); exposure 4 (child-safety and emotional-support chatbots touch a very broad consumer/youth surface); trajectory decelerating because the anchoring safety subfield's velocity is negative (-5.4%/qtr) — the foundation is fading even as the application spreads.

verification.prior_coverage: Every named component of this risk is already in active public discussion: LLM-as-judge / auditor-judge safety benchmarks for emotional-support chatbots (e.g. ESC-Judge) and for child safety (Safe-Child-LLM, SproutBench, YouthSafe, MinorBench) all exist publicly; the reliability, bias, gameability and especially cross-lingual degradation of LLM judges is a documented, named research concern (BabelJudge, multilingual-judge reliability work, 'One Token to Fool LLM-as-a-Judge', LLM-as-a-Judge SoK); and the child-safety carve-out from federal AI preemption under the recent executive order plus the CAISI evaluation mandate in the draft Great American AI Act are widely covered by major law firms and policy outlets. The specific cross-domain seam — that judge-based child-safety benchmarks become a regulatory reference standard whose oversight foundation is under-scrutinized — is not stated verbatim anywhere I found, but each link in the chain is mainstream.

verification.disputed_claims: Two load-bearing claims are contested. (1) 'Scrutiny of scalable-oversight / judge reliability is shrinking' — public evidence points the OTHER way: cross-lingual judge degradation, judge gameability ('optimize to the letter of the criteria'), and judge security are actively growing, named research areas in 2025-2026 (BabelJudge, multilingual-judge reliability studies, LLM-as-a-Judge security SoK, One Token to Fool), and major labs continued scalable-oversight work into April 2026. So the premise that the methods community that would catch judge deficiencies is 'shrinking' is not supported and is implicitly disputed by this body of work. (2) The 'Scalable Oversight -5.4%/qtr deceleration' is the tool's own proprietary signal, not independently corroborated; the risk text itself concedes it is an attention signal, not a causal claim. (3) The legal-crystallization step depends on the draft Great American AI Act and CAISI mandate, which lawyers note are not yet law and CAISI's role is framed as voluntary guidelines/standards, not a binding benchmark mandate.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Low novelty, low-to-moderate confidence. The constituent elements are all already prominent in public discussion: judge-based emotional-support AND child-safety benchmarks exist, the 'tune to satisfy the judge rather than the safety property' failure mode is explicitly described in the LLM-as-judge literature, multilingual/cross-lingual judge degradation is a named and actively researched problem, and the child-safety preemption carve-out plus CAISI's evaluation role are widely covered. The one fresh element is the specific framing connecting these into a regulatory-lock-in seam — that is at most partially fresh packaging, not an unsurfaced risk. Critically, the risk's central premise (that scrutiny of judge reliability is DECREASING) runs against the public evidence, which shows that scrutiny is increasing; the -5.4%/qtr figure is an uncorroborated proprietary signal explicitly labeled non-causal. The legal trigger is also pre-enactment and CAISI's mandate is currently described as voluntary. Recommend briefing this only as a 'watch the seam' item, not a novel risk, and only if the 'scrutiny is shrinking' claim is dropped or rewritten, since it is contradicted by current literature.

foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.

**CURRENT-STATE BRIEF — as of late June 2026**
*Flagged topics: Agentic Behavior Monitoring; Scalable Oversight*

**May 5, 2026 — US government model evaluation (updates standing context).** The standing context lists CAISI evaluation partnerships with "Google, Microsoft, and xAI" as recent. The verified record: on May 5, 2026, NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI) announced pre-deployment testing agreements with Google DeepMind, Microsoft, and xAI, expanding its frontier model evaluation program to five major labs — building on CAISI's previous partnerships with OpenAI and Anthropic. The agreements cover pre-deployment evaluations, post-deployment assessments, and related research on frontier AI systems — directly relevant to agentic monitoring and oversight tooling.

**May 7, 2026 — EU AI Act high-risk timeline shifted (corrects standing context).** The context says high-risk obligations carry an Aug 2, 2026 deadline with a delay merely "proposed." That has now advanced: on May 7, 2026, the Council and the European Parliament reached provisional agreement on the Digital Omnibus; high-risk obligations under Annex III now apply from 2 December 2027, and high-risk AI embedded in regulated Annex I products from 2 August 2028. Formal adoption is still pending, but watermarking/synthetic-content disclosure goes live 2 December 2026. The deal also introduces a new Article 5 prohibition on AI-generated non-consensual intimate imagery and CSAM.

**June 4, 2026 — Federal preemption draft (confirms standing context, still pre-introduction).** On June 4, Reps. Jay Obernolte (R-CA) and Lori Trahan (D-MA) released a 269-page bipartisan discussion draft, the Great American Artificial Intelligence Act of 2026. It remains a draft soliciting feedback, not introduced or voted. Beyond the three-year build-stage preemption, it is relevant to oversight: it would require the country's most powerful AI developers to submit to semi-annual evaluations.

**Jan 1, 2026 — California SB 53** remains in effect as the standing context states; no change verified.
